hints: Only the 2♥ and the 12 face cards (K,Q,J) matter — 13 cards.
The condition is that the 2♥ comes before all 12 face cards.
By symmetry the 2♥ is first among these 13 with probability 1/13.
solution: The only relevant cards are the $2\heartsuit$ and the $12$ face cards. We need the $2\heartsuit$ to precede all of them, i.e. be first among these $13$ equally likely orderings: probability $\tfrac1{13}.$

hints: Rewrite max(3S,15)=15+3·max(S−5,0).
That's 15 bonds plus 3 calls at K=5.
solution: $\max(3S_T,15)=15+3\max(S_T-5,0)$, replicated by $15$ bonds and $3$ calls struck at $5$. Its price is $15(0.9)+3(4.2)=13.5+12.6=26.1.$

hints: Pick the pair's rank & suits, then 3 distinct other ranks each with one suit.
solution: Choose the pair's rank ($13$) and $2$ suits ($\binom42=6$); then $3$ distinct other ranks ($\binom{12}3$) each with one of $4$ suits. So $P=\dfrac{13\cdot6\cdot\binom{12}3\cdot4^3}{\binom{52}5}=\dfrac{1098240}{2598960}=\dfrac{352}{833}.$

hints: Match P(value=v) for one roll with P(sum=v) for two rolls, for v=2..6.
This forces p_2=p_1², p_3=2p_1³, p_4=5p_1⁴, p_5=14p_1⁵, p_6=42p_1⁶ (Catalan numbers).
Normalization Σp=1 gives coefficients 1,1,2,5,14,42 summing to 65.
solution: Equating one-roll and two-roll probabilities for $2,\dots,6$ gives $p_2=p_1^2$, $p_3=2p_1^3$, $p_4=5p_1^4$, $p_5=14p_1^5$, $p_6=42p_1^6$ — the Catalan numbers. The normalization $\sum p_i=1$ reads $\sum_{k=1}^6 c_k p_1^k=1$ with $(c_k)=(1,1,2,5,14,42)$, whose sum is $65.$

hints: If x is the age at death, birth year = 29x and death year = 30x.
Need death year <1945 and birth year in the 1800s: x=64 ⇒ born 1856, died 1920.
His 1900 birthday age = 1900−1856 = 44.
solution: If $x$ is his age at death, the birth year is $29x$ and the death year $30x$. Requiring a death before $1945$ and a 19th-century birth gives $x=64$: born $1856$, died $1920$. On his $1900$ birthday he turned $1900-1856=44.$

hints: Perimeter P = AC+AB+BC; differentiate.
= −1 units/sec (lengths irrelevant).
solution: The perimeter's rate is the sum of the side rates: $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=3+(-2)+(-2)=-1$ units per second (the current lengths don't matter).

hints: This is symmetric; both reroll below a threshold k, aiming to win not to maximize value.
Indifference at the threshold yields k²+k−1=0.
solution: By symmetry both players reroll when below a threshold $k$. The optimal $k$ is where you're indifferent between keeping $k$ and rerolling against the opponent's same strategy, which reduces to $k^2+k-1=0$. The positive root is $k=\dfrac{\sqrt5-1}{2}\approx0.618.$

hints: Each roll is 'prime' (2,3,5) with probability 1/2.
With equal rolls each would lead with probability 1/2; the extra roll breaks ties symmetrically.
P(player 2 leads) = 1/2.
solution: Each roll is prime with probability $\tfrac12$. With the same number of rolls, by symmetry each player leads with equal probability and ties split evenly; giving player 2 one extra roll exactly balances this, so $P(\text{player 2 has more})=\tfrac12.$

hints: Each element is in A∩B with probability 1/4 (independently).
solution: Each of the $20$ elements is in $A\cap B$ independently with probability $\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac14$, so $N\sim\mathrm{Binom}(20,\tfrac14)$ with $\mathbb{E}[N]=5$ and $\mathrm{Var}(N)=20\cdot\tfrac14\cdot\tfrac34=\tfrac{15}4$. Thus $\dfrac{\mathrm{Var}(N)}{\mathbb{E}[N]}=\dfrac{15/4}{5}=\dfrac34.$

hints: Bob's gain picking x: lose Y if x>Y, gain x if x<Y.
Maximize: x=33 or 34 give 3366/200 = 16.83.
solution: If Bob picks $x$, he loses $Y$ when $x>Y$ and gains $x$ when $x<Y$: $\mathbb{E}[Z_x]=-\dfrac{(x-1)x}{200}+\dfrac{x(100-x)}{100}=\dfrac{x(201-3x)}{200}$. Maximizing over integers gives $x=33$ or $34$, both yielding $\dfrac{3366}{200}=16.83.$

hints: There are C(12,4) candidate 4-circuits; use an indicator for each.
A 4-circuit needs all C(4,2)=6 wires active: probability (1/2)⁶.
solution: There are $\binom{12}{4}=495$ sets of $4$ bulbs, each forming a complete circuit iff all $\binom42=6$ connecting wires are active, probability $(\tfrac12)^6=\tfrac1{64}$. By linearity, $\mathbb{E}=\dfrac{495}{64}.$

hints: WLOG cards are 1,2,3; the strategy can only use the difference of the first two cards (n is unknown).
After flipping: on difference +2 keep (it's the max); on a −1 difference take the third; otherwise stay/decide to maximize.
Averaging the six orderings gives 14/6=7/3=n+4/3, so c=4/3.
solution: Take the cards as $1,2,3$ (shift by $n-1$). Since $n$ is unknown, decisions can only use the observed difference of the first two cards. Flipping the first card (staying yields only $2$), then for each ordered pair choosing optimally on the difference: outcomes are $2,3,3,1,3,2$ for $(1,2),(2,3),(2,1),(3,2),(1,3),(3,1)$, averaging $\tfrac{14}{6}=\tfrac73$. So the expected payout is $n+\tfrac43$, giving $c=\tfrac43.$
